A Peruvian citizen with voter identification for the 2021 election: Peru’s bishops appealed for honesty, transparency and consistency in the 2026 elections, after eight presidents since 2018.
US Agency for International Development



The Holy See published the programme for Pope Leo’s apostolic journey to four African countries on 13-23 April.\xa0 
Besides meetings with civil authorities and local bishops, in Algeria the Pope will visit Augustinian communities and the Great Mosque in Algiers, hold a “Meeting for Peace” in the capital of Cameroon’s crisis-ridden North West region Bamenda, pray the Rosary at the Mama Muxima Shrine in eastern Angola and visit the memorial to victims of explosions on 7 March 2021 which killed more than 100 people the city of Bata, Equatorial Guinea.\xa0 
He will also celebrate Masses and visit hospitals, schools and other Church-run institutions, including the Leo XIV Campus of Equatorial Guinea’s National University. 
In Angola, Bishop Maurício Agostinho Camuto of Caxito warned leaders against politicising the Pope’s visit.\xa0 
“Let us not think only of political gain from his presence, but rather seek, above all, spiritual, moral, and social benefit from his teachings,” he said, warning that previous popes “called for peace, reconciliation and justice, but Angolans, especially politicians, let the messages enter one ear and leave through the other”.\xa0 
“We cannot repeat the same errors with Leo XIV. We need the courage to listen and to obey,” he said.

A guild of southern African Catholic women called for governments to respond to gender-based violence as the region struggles with high cases of femicide. The St Anne’s Sodality, which represents more than 18,000 women from 26 dioceses, said greater participation of women in the leadership of the Church would help address issues affecting them.\xa0 
“Many women who suffer violence cannot afford counselling,” said Lindiwe Zondi, national president of St Anne’s Sodality, during commemorations in South Africa last week for International Women’s Day.\xa0 
The St Anne’s Sodality is an affiliate of the World Union of Catholic Women’s Organisations, and Zondi said that “through the sodality they find a space where they can speak, be listened to, and receive support from other women”.\xa0She insisted that “every woman and girl, regardless of background or ability, must live with dignity and freedom”.\xa0 
“If women are more involved in leadership, it will help them to be recognised and contribute more visibly in the Church,” she said.

Catholic bishops across East Africa offered condolences after floods killed 100 people in Ethiopia, nearly 70 in Kenya and an unknown number in Uganda.\xa0Rescue teams were still searching for missing persons early this week while thousands of people remained displaced.\xa0 
The Ethiopian government announced a three-day mourning period, following the devastating landslides and floods hit that Gamo Zone in the south of the country on 11 March. 
“We grieve as well for the many families who have been displaced and now face great suffering in the aftermath of this disaster,” said a statement from the Ethiopian Catholic bishops’ conference. The Church supported relief efforts but also urged citizens to collaborate for immediate humanitarian assistance.\xa0 
In Kenya, churches opened their doors to accommodate people left homeless by floods on 6 March that turned roads in Nairobi into small rivers, sweeping away cars and people and submerging homes and businesses.

At least 300 catechists are to receive training in Ethiopia to serve some of the country’s most remote areas, amid a shortage of priests and surging religious violence in parts of the country. \xa0
Aid to the Church in Need (ACN) announced its funding for the initiative last week.\xa0The catechists will receive theology and teaching training to help the Church grow and respond to crises affecting the country.\xa0The catechists are “the primary focus of a new training initiative in the apostolic vicariate of Hawassa, a vast region”, ACN reported.\xa0 
“Given that this area is roughly one-third the size of Germany and features many inaccessible villages, these lay leaders are indispensable for the transmission of the Catholic faith,” it said.

The Catholic bishops of the Benin Republic appealed for calm ahead of elections in April. In a statement issued at the end of its four-day plenary assembly, the Episcopal Conference of Benin said that diversity in opinions should be a “source of strength” for the country’s democracy and called for unity amid “diverse interests and political differences in the country”.\xa0 
President Patrice Talon, currently serving his second and final constitutional term, has reportedly denied any intention to amend the constitution to seek re-election. He faced an attempted coup earlier in the year, but received support from the Nigerian military to suppress it.

A group of 35 Nigerian bishops visited the Pope on 14 March, following a visit by another group of 37 on 6 March, completing their ad limina visit to the Holy See – their first since Pope Leo’s election. The meeting reportedly included discussions of Nigeria’s security crisis and widespread killings and abductions, which campaigners say target Christians.

One in seven Christians worldwide suffers the effects of persecution or violence, according to Archbishop Ettore Balestrero, the Holy See’s Permanent Observer to the United Nations in Geneva. \xa0\xa0
“Almost 400 million Christians worldwide face persecution or violence, making them the most persecuted religious community in the world,” he said.\xa0Speaking on 3 March at the event “Standing with Persecuted Christians: Defending the Faith and Christian Values”, Archbishop Balestrero added: “Almost 5,000 Christians were killed for their faith in 2025, which equates to an average of 13 per day.”\xa0 
He said that according to the Observatory on Intolerance and Discrimination Against Christians in Europe, in 2024 there were 2,211 incidents affecting Christians in Europe. \xa0
“These include prosecutions for silent prayer near abortion facilities or for quoting a Bible verse on social issues,” he said. Balestrero said these were “not superficial acts” but “serious violations of the rights of Christians, perpetrated by the very authorities who are charged with the duty of respecting, protecting and promoting the human rights of all”. He observed: “Attacks on Christians are attacks on the Cross itself.”

The prefect of the Dicastery for the Eastern Churches Cardinal Claudio Gugerotti warned that despite efforts to find peace in the Holy Land, “arms continue to fire, people continue to die, lands remain contested, and Christians continue to emigrate in order to survive”. \xa0
In a report on the Good Friday Collection for the Holy Places, Cardinal Gugerotti said its support is “vital” to “help our brothers and sisters who live in extreme danger, so that they may endure one more day, rediscover hope and begin again”. He urged Christians to give, lest they “risk remaining inert before a world in flames – and thus complicit in its destruction”. \xa0
He continued: “The faithful who remain in the Holy Places know that through your support – and sometimes only through it – even if security cannot yet be guaranteed, schools can reopen, new homes can be built and care can be provided where destruction has been total.” \xa0
On Monday, Pope Leo spoke to Mahmoud Abbas, president of the Palestinian Authority, about “the alarming developments in the conflict in the Middle East and the living conditions of the Palestinian people”. The Holy See said that during the telephone call, the Pope reaffirmed its commitment to achieving peace.

A 22-year-old Catholic farm labourer was found hanged in a farm outbuilding outside the city of Sargodha in Punjab. According to reports, Marqas Masih’s body showed possible signs of torture. \xa0
Bishop Samson Shukardin of Hyderabad, president of the Catholic Bishops’ Conference of Pakistan, and Naeem Yousaf Gill, executive secretary of the National Commission for Justice and Peace, issued a statement urging the “authorities to conduct a full investigation into the incident, bring the facts to light and ensure that justice is delivered to this vulnerable community”. \xa0
Gill told Aid to the Church in Need: “The authorities should not leave Christians to be treated as second class citizens. We should be treated as equal citizens. We demand a fully transparent investigation.” Police arrested Muhammad Bashrat, the farm landlord, who reported the death to Masih’s family, and another man, Mohammad Moshan.

The Catholic Bishops’ Conference of Myanmar (CBCM) designated 26 March as a day of prayer for peace, particularly in Myanmar and the Middle East. In a letter addressed to “the children of God”, the CBCM president Cardinal Charles Maung Bo of Yangon asked for prayers “that God gives his peace to the world and to Myanmar and that there will be mutual understanding and progress in unity”.

The Archbishop of Tokyo Cardinal Isao Kikuchi apologised for the failings of the Church in a statement for the Day of Prayer and Penance for Victims of Sexual Abuse, which the Church in Japan marked on 6 March.\xa0 
“We sincerely apologise for the Church’s sin of committing violence against life, a gift from God, and of disregarding human dignity,” he said.\xa0While noting efforts to reform his diocese’s safeguarding, Cardinal Kikuchi said “institutional limitations based on the Church’s internal history have not yet been overcome”.\xa0 
The statement, published in February, said the Japanese bishops’ conference was working with the Pontifical Commission for the Protection of Minors to improve its safeguarding mechanisms.\xa0The conference said it introduced the 6 March commemoration “in line with the wishes of Pope Francis”.

Peru needs “authorities characterised by honesty, transparency and consistency between their words and actions”, the country’s bishops said in a pre-election message.\xa0 
The statement published on 15 March by the permanent council of the Peruvian bishops’ conference was intended to “help bring about change in the situation we are experiencing as a nation”, the bishops said, expressing concern as “deep social fragmentation” ahead of elections on 12 April.\xa0 
Peru has had eight presidents since 2018 amid chronic political and economic instability, with polling last week suggesting scattered support for election candidates and widespread disillusionment.\xa0 
The bishops’ continued: “The persistence of deep social, economic, and territorial inequalities, where many people continue to live in conditions of poverty, exclusion and lack of opportunity, is difficult to understand in a country blessed with natural and cultural wealth, multiethnic diversity and the generosity and solidarity of its people. This challenges our national conscience.”\xa0 
They said the election should be a moment of “ethical discernment” to allow the country “to strengthen its democratic institutions”.

The Archdiocese of Port-au-Prince appealed for the release of Fr Jean Robert Louis, who was kidnapped on 5 February. A statement published a month after his abduction asked for his freedom “so he can resume his pastoral mission” caring for prisoners in the Haitian capital.\xa0It said abducting people for ransom was “a direct offence to their fundamental dignity and a grave sin against human brotherhood”.\xa0 
Powerful gangs control much of Port-au-Prince and fighting between them and against the government has subjected Haiti to social and economic crisis since 2020.\xa0Elections are scheduled for this year but last month Bishop Pierre-André Dumas of Anse-à-Veau warned the violence would “make it impossible to organise transparent, honest, democratic and inclusive elections”.

The Holy See’s undercover dialogue with the Cuban government emerged briefly into the daylight on 28 February, when Pope Leo received in audience the Cuban foreign minister, Bruno Rodríguez Parrilla. Later on social media, the minister expressed his “deep gratitude for the honour” of the audience. 
Earlier in the month the Pope had expressed his “deep concern” for the worsening relations between the United States and Cuba and called for a “sincere and effective” dialogue to resolved the situation. \xa0
On 12 March the Cuban government announced its decision to release 51 prisoners, which it said was taken “in the spirit of good will, and close and easy relations between the Cuban state and the Vatican, with which there has historically been communication about the processes of review and release of people deprived of freedom”. It also mentioned “the proximity of the religious celebrations of Holy Week”.\xa0 
Shortly after this statement President Miguel Diaz-Canel, announced that there had been “conversations” between Cuban officials and US representatives.\xa0
Diaz-Canel also mentioned “international factors that had facilitated this exchange”, which is interpreted as a reference to Church mediation, particularly as the US chargé d’affaires in Havana visited the Vatican in mid-February and met Archbishop Paul Gallagher, the Vatican secretary for relations with states.

The United States’ government’s mass detention and mass deportation policies are a “grave moral evil”, Bishop Mark Seitz of El Paso, Texas said in a pastoral letter published on 15 March.\xa0 
In the letter titled “A Pastoral Message on Mass Detention and Mass Deportations in El Paso”, Seitz calls on those who work in federal immigration enforcement to follow the Gospel instead of the orders of the Trump administration.\xa0 
“No one has to obey an immoral order. I implore all involved to carefully discern the moral requirements of the Gospel at this moment with integrity and honesty. When we take off our masks and encounter each other as neighbours, we can reclaim our common dignity,” Seitz said.\xa0 
A spokesperson for the diocese said the bishop’s message is the first of its kind since the Trump administration began its mass deportation campaign in 2025. The pastoral letter is addressed to those who work in immigration enforcement and to members of the immigrant community in the border region.\xa0
“In recent months, I have heard your fears, sufferings and worries about deportation,” Seitz said.\xa0“I have heard the stories about families being separated and of members being taken away from our community.”

The Archdiocese of Detroit announced several new initiatives aimed at preventing sexual abuse. In a pastoral letter dated 11 March, Archbishop Edward Weisenburger said all priests and employees will be required to be fingerprinted. \xa0
Weisenburger also said the archdiocese plans to hire a supervisor who will “monitor priests or deacons who have been removed from ministry” and coordinate with local and US federal authorities.\xa0 
“When I was installed as your archbishop one year ago, I accepted not only many joyful responsibilities, but also a most solemn one. Among the more serious obligations entrusted to me is addressing the wounds caused by clergy sexual abuse, along with strengthening the Church’s commitment to accountability and protection,” Weisenburger wrote in his letter, titled “Rebuilding Trust: A Pastoral Message on Abuse, Reform and Hope”. \xa0
While saying that the Church has made important strides on preventing sexual abuse, the archbishop added that “we must commit ourselves to ongoing accountability, transparency and the unwavering protection of those entrusted to our care – especially God’s beloved little ones”.\xa0 
Weisenburger said the archdiocese has been reviewing all abuse cases dating back to the 1920s.\xa0“Each case was carefully examined in order to clearly identify those that involved credible allegations of sexual abuse of minors or vulnerable adults, ensuring that no allegation was overlooked and no victim deprived of justice or an opportunity for care,” the archbishop said.

The body of a 56-year-old French priest missing since he was informed of a possible sexual abuse inquiry against him two years ago was found in a river in Brittany.\xa0 
The empty car and telephone of Fr Christophe Guégan, parish priest in Ploërmel, were found in January 2024 near a remote canoeing spot about 30km north of Lorient, known as the Devil’s Rocks.\xa0He was staying at a convent near the canoeing spot and was to celebrate Mass in morning.\xa0When he did not appear, the nuns informed the police.\xa0 
Fr Guégan’s body was found along the Ellé River five kilometres further downstream. Prior to his disappearance, he had recently been informed of the inquiry into “possible acts of sexual touching” after a complaint filed in Lyon.\xa0 
Bishop Raymond Centènet of Vannes announced his death on 12 March, expressing “profound emotion”. His funeral was to take place on 18 March in the Basilica of Saint Anne in Auray.\xa0Bishop Centènet said the priest died “under still-unexplained circumstances”. Police in Lorient suspect suicide.\xa0 
Shocked parishioners described Fr Guégan as “dynamic, calm and level-headed”. They said he had inspired several vocations and had “lots of plans” for the future.

Archbishop Laurent Ulrich of Paris denounced vandalism of churches in the French capital after relics of St Vincent de Paul and St Thérèse of Lisieux were stolen and the altar of the Church of St Roch profaned.\xa0 
A piece of cloth worn by St Vincent de Paul was taken on 7 March from a church in northern Paris dedicated to him, as was an unspecified relic of St Thérèse of Lisieux.\xa0The reliquaries were left at the scene.\xa0
At the baroque St Roch church in central Paris, vandals tried but failed to steal a heavy tabernacle on 8 March. The next day, heavy stones from the crumbling facade tumbled down to the pavement. Nobody was injured.\xa0 
In a statement, Archbishop Ulrich offered “his prayers and closeness” to the two parishes. He said he was confident that police were investigating the cases “to preserve the lives of the communities who pray there, as well as the works of art and religious artifacts that these churches contain”.

An excommunicated group of former Poor Clares left their monastery in Belorado in northern Spain last week, shortly before the implementation of a judicial order for their eviction.\xa0 
The Archbishop of Burgos Mario Iceta published videos after they had left, appearing to show they had left the 3,000 sq ft convent dirty. It showed mice scuttling down alleys and behind furniture and pots of leftover food.\xa0
In a statement, a spokesman for the Archdiocese of Burgos said: “The fourteenth-century parchments which contain the bull of foundation of the monastery used to be kept in a wardrobe. When we entered, we found [the ex-nuns] had taken the wardrobe and placed the parchments below some blankets.” 
He continued: “They took the whole kitchen. It was pulled out.\xa0They also took china and a portable electric light generator.” The spokesman alleged that items were missing from the convent church, saying: “Not a single liturgical ornament was left. They took a made-to-measure oak cupboard and none of the statues were left in the display cabinet.”\xa0
The schismatic community published a video of the convent after that issued by Iceta, showing corridors inside the building with gleaming wooden floors.

News Briefing: Church in the World


Patrick Hudson, Ellen Teague, Bess Twiston Davies, Marko Phiri, Tom Heneghan 

                        06 June 2025, The Tablet
                    


Prev



Next





























The Lonza valley in Switzerland, where the village of Blatten was demolished by an avalanche. The local bishop cited Isaiah 54:10: “For the mountains may depart and the hills be removed, but my steadfast love shall not depart from you.”\xa0
Michael Buholzer / Keystone / Associated Press / Alamy



The Egyptian government denied that a controversial court ruling, declaring the land around the historic St Catherine’s Monastery in the Sinai Peninsula state property, posed any threat to the community which has inhabited the site since the sixth-century. \xa0
The Greek Orthodox Church reacted with concern to last week’s ruling, as the monastery had enjoyed protected legal status since it was built on the site of the burning bush. 
In a call on 30 May with Greek Prime Minister Kyriakos Mitsotakis, the Egyptian President Abdel Fattah al-Sisi said Cairo was “fully committed to preserving the unique and sacred religious status of Saint Catherine’s Monastery, and ensuring it is not violated”. Mitsotakis emphasised the importance of “preserving the pilgrimage and Greek Orthodox character of the monastery”. A Greek delegation was due to visit Egypt next week, the Athens government reported. 
Archbishop Ieronymos of Athens had called the court ruling “scandalous” and an infringement on religious freedoms.\xa0The Greek Orthodox patriarchates in Jerusalem and Istanbul and the Ecumenical Patriarchate of Constantinople also called on the Egyptian government to respect longstanding tradition and agreements with the world’s oldest continually-inhabited Christian monastery. \xa0
The ruling came amid a government development project to boost visitor numbers to the region, which is popular with pilgrims and tourists.

The United Nations reported worsening humanitarian crises in Sudan’s civil war between government forces and the paramilitary Rapid Support Forces (RSF).\xa0The UN secretary-general’s spokesperson Stéphane Dujarric de la Rivière described dire conditions in the town of El Fasher and the Abu Shouk refugee camp, which are besieged by the RSF.\xa0 
According to the International Organisation for Migration, around 2,800 people, mostly women and children, fled the renewed fighting in last week in South Kordofan, as the RSF pursued plans to establish a parallel government in the Darfur and Kordofan regions.\xa0
The UN said a cholera epidemic in Khartoum State had worsened, with the number of suspected cases increasing by 80 per cent to over 8,500 in the last two weeks.\xa0Although the World Health Organisation has delivered some medical supplies to Khartoum hospitals, “much more is needed to contain the epidemic”, Dujarric said.

Church authorities in Kenya suspended operations in Kerio Valley after the killing of Fr Allois Cheruiyot Bett, amid reports of widespread insecurity across the Rift Valley region.\xa0
Bett, a priest attached to St Mulumba Church in Tot, in the Diocese of Eldoret, was shot dead by suspected bandits as he returned to the parish headquarters after presiding over a Jumuiya, or community Mass, in the valley. His death provoked protests in Eldoret ahead of his burial on 3 June.\xa0 
Among the Church facilities closed was the Chesongoch Mission Hospital, run by the Benedictine Missionary Sisters.\xa0Sr Rosa Pascal, the head of the mission, said dangers in the area was causing mental, emotional and psychological trauma to the sisters, making it difficult for them to carry out essential services.\xa0The mission employed around 100 people, including nurses and doctors, but many had fled the area due to the insecurity. \xa0
The Church said the closure was necessary to protect employees, as well as the sisters and citizens seeking treatment.\xa0The wider suspension was intended to pressure government into acting against the region’s insecurity.

Church leaders in African must speak out against the sexual abuse of women religious if they are to be “stewards of human dignity”, a Zambian nun said.\xa0 
“Sexual abuse represents a profound and hallowing failure to protect the most vulnerable members of our communities,” said Sr Linah Siabana in an address to the general meeting of the Conference of Major Superiors of Africa and Madagascar on 24 May.\xa0“We bear a collective responsibility to ensure that our conduct consistently safeguards the rights and well-being of those entrusted to our care.”\xa0 
Siabana, of the Missionary Sisters of Our Lady of Africa, who specialises in mental health work with refugees, said Church leaders must “move beyond protectionism” to support women Religious.

South Africa still feels the effects of apartheid decades after it ended in 1994 in the huge divide between rich and poor, Cardinal Wilfrid Napier OFM said last week.\xa0 
The 84-year-old former Archbishop of Durban made the remarks amid intensified international interest in race relations, after the US government granted asylum to a group of white South Africans who said they faced persecution.\xa0During a meeting at the White House on 21 May, President Donald Trump alleged to South Africa’s President Cyril Ramaphosa that a “white genocide” was taking place, a claim condemned by religious leaders and rights activists.\xa0 
“The structures of apartheid that were put into place cannot be reversed,” Cardinal Napier said in a news agency interview, though he noted the failures of black-majority governments to address the lasting socio-economic effects.\xa0“While racial segregation legislation has gone, removing legislation does not remove the reality,” he said.

Dozens of people died last week in a wave of attacks in Nigeria’s Middle Belt by militant members of the Fulani herder community. More than 40 people were killed in separate attacks in Benue State. 
Nomadic Fulani herders have long clashed there with settled farmers, many of whom are Christian, over access to land and resources.\xa0The Diocese of Makurdi reported that farmers were killed on their land by Fulani jihadists who fired indiscriminately on villages.\xa0
Fr Oliver Ortese, chairman of the International Advisory Board for Makurdi Diocese, criticised the failure of security services to respond to the first attacks in Jimba.\xa0“There is a military post where the Nigerian army personnel are kept by the government where this incident happened,” he said. “Were the soldiers sleeping while these shootings were going on?” \xa0
He reported widespread panic and “many residents fled their homes in search of safety”. Fr Ortese reflected: “They are creating humanitarian crises as those who survive are moved into camps where they become beggars to eek out a living. You cannot imagine the reality we live in here. This is horror, this is terror.”\xa0 
The Bishop of Makurdi Wilfred Anagbe has testified to the US Congress about religious violence in Nigeria. 
Elsewhere, at least 200 people died in floods in Niger State last weekend. The rural town of Mokwa, west of Nigeria’s capital Abuja and a major trading and transportation hub for northern Nigerian farmers, suffered the worst of the flood. At least 500 households were destroyed and two roads and two bridges collapsed after heavy rainfall submerged huge areas of the town.

Bishops condemned the killing of soldiers in Benin by alleged jihadist militias, decrying worsening violence in the country which has claimed thousands of lives.\xa0More than 50 soldiers were killed in April in government efforts to quell a longstanding Islamist insurgency.\xa0 
“The bishops of Benin express their deepest sympathy to the entire nation and to the grieving families of our soldiers who fell in the line of duty,” a bishops’ conference statement said. “We also pray for their brothers and sisters in arms who are still on the front lines, that the Lord may be their shield and their protection.”\xa0 
It continued: “Aware of the sacrifices made to preserve peace and security in our country, we pray for the eternal rest of these heroes.”

Churches in Sierra Leone issued guidelines to limit parish activities after the government confirmed the outbreak of mpox, a viral disease that has claimed the lives of thousands of people in parts of Africa.\xa0
The World Health Organisation announced in April that the Democratic Republic of the Congo, Uganda, Sierra Leone and Burundi had been hit by mpox, with thousands of deaths reported since last year.\xa0 
“As a local Church, we need to do everything possible to protect ourselves from the disease and also contain its spread by carefully observing the guidelines announced by health authorities,” said Archbishop Edward Tamba Charles of Freetown in a statement last week.\xa0 
The guidelines advise avoiding physical interactions, with the country’s health officials noting that the slightest contact such as a handshake could prove infectious. “I assure you of my prayers for God’s blessing and protection, especially from mpox,” Archbishop Tamba said.

Malta’s Cardinal Mario Grech called the shortage of vocation a possible “grace from God” that could lead to the laity helping administer the sacraments. \xa0
Speaking to The Times of Malta, Grech said: “Some are scandalised by these words, because it could prompt the church to recognise and utilise the diverse gifts present among all Christians rather than concentrating power solely within the clergy”. \xa0
Grech, the secretary general of the Synod of Bishops, said the Church must understand that the Holy Spirit was not the exclusive domain of priests, even if some clergy resisted the idea. While priestly ministry remains vital, he said, there is plenty the laity can do in the Church, outside administrative tasks. 
He cited an example from Switzerland where a lay couple effectively run a parish.\xa0As priestly visits are infrequent, the couple preside over christenings, funerals and weddings, instead of the priest. \xa0
Grech said the aim of the Synod of Synodality was to move the Church away from clergy-dominated leadership to give the laity more say in decisions made in parishes, dioceses and the Vatican.

Bishop Jean-Marie Lovey of Sion said, “I weep for Blatten”, after the village in the mountainous Valais region of southern Switzerland was swept under a bursting glacier softened by climate change.\xa0The Swiss bishops conference said it was “deeply shocked” by the disaster and urged the country’s faithful to support Blatten’s residents – who were safely evacuated beforehand – both materially and spiritually.\xa0 
In his message, the local bishop quoted God’s words from Isaiah 54:10: “For the mountains may depart and the hills be removed, but my steadfast love shall not depart from you.”\xa0
The frozen Birch Glacier above Blatten burst on 28 May, covering the village below with mud and rocks sometimes 100 metres deep. The 300 inhabitants were evacuated a week earlier when geologists noted the glacier was weakening.\xa0 
Several hundred thousand cubic metres of cliff above the village remain unstable with summer temperatures threatening further melting.\xa0 Switzerland’s glaciers, the most numerous in Europe, are expected to continue to thaw in decades to come, increasing the risk of landslide and threatening other mountain villages.

Adult baptisms in Belgium continued an upward trend seen in other European countries, but other statistics showed decline for a Church beset by secularisation and the abuse scandal.\xa0The 536 adults and adolescents baptised at the 2025 Easter vigil were 48 per cent more than in 2024, whose 362 baptisms were already an increase over 260 in 2023, official figures show.\xa0
But other indicators were less optimistic. There were 29,769 infant baptisms in Belgium last year, 5,057 fewer than in 2023, and falls of nearly 10 per cent in numbers making their first communion and 7 per cent for confirmation in the same period.\xa0Church weddings dropped by 6.5 per cent from 2023 to 2024, though a less sharp decline than the 24 per cent drop between 2022 and 2023.\xa0 
“Requesting and receiving the sacraments is increasingly a conscious choice,” said a message from the Belgian bishops accompanying the official figures.\xa0“For some time now, we have been witnessing a gradual transition from a cultural Christianity to a Christianity of conviction.”

The Cathedral of Notre-Dame de Paris, reopened last December after five years of reconstruction following its 2019 fire, dedicated one of its chapels to the Churches of the East.\xa0 Eight icons adorn the walls of the chapel officially opened on 28 May and entrusted to the aid society L’Œuvre d’Orient, based in the French capital.
“The cathedral is Catholic and therefore universal, open to all,” said Mgr Olivier Ribadeau-Dumas, rector of the cathedral.\xa0Quoting John Paul II, he said: “The Christian breathes with the two lungs of the Church, Western and Eastern.”\xa0
The icons, representing saints who founded the great Oriental churches, were blessed during a Mass presided by Cardinal Louis Raphaël Sako, the Chaldean Patriarch of Babylon. There are 23 Eastern Catholic churches that celebrate Eastern rites but are in union with the Pope.

The council of the city of Lisbon agreed to name a park after Pope Francis to commemorate his visit to Portugal in 2023 for World Youth Day. The 100-hectare Tejo Park spanning Lisbon and the municipality of Loures, in greater Lisbon, was created on a former landfill site as part of Lisbon’s preparations for the youth rally in August 2023. “The dream is for it to be a green park, used by people,” said Carlos Moedas, the Mayor of Lisbon.

Salesian missionaries in Haiti reported providing meals to 2,300 adolescent boys and girls daily to ensure they have at least one healthy meal a day. The order works with the humanitarian organisation Rise Against Hunger in three missionary centres across the island, including the Vincent Foundation centre near Cap-Haïtien which provides regular meals and allows young people to socialise in safety.\xa0 
Church workers have faced increasing difficulties in responding to the hunger crisis amid gang violence following the assassination of President Jovenel Moïse in 2021. One million Haitians – nearly a tenth of the population – have been displaced from their homes. \xa0Around 5.7million face acute food insecurity, according to the UN.

Pope Leo XIV appointed a Nicaraguan-born priest an auxiliary bishop of the Archdiocese of Newark in New Jersey. \xa0
Fr Pedro Bismarck Chau, who is bilingual in Spanish and English, was born in July 1964 in the Nicaraguan capital Managua.\xa0Ordained in 2008, Chau has parish experience in New Jersey, where he has served as assistant director of vocations for the archdiocese, as chaplain for pastoral ministry with the deaf and in missionary outreach to Latin Americans in the United States. \xa0
The Archbishop of Newark Cardinal Joseph Tobin said Fr Chau’s “deep faith, considerable pastoral experience, and love for the people of God will be a great blessing to our local Church”. An estimated 1.03 million Catholics live in New Jersey.

Archbishop Robert Casey of Cincinnati travelled to a town in rural Ohio to celebrate Mass with a community whose historic church was destroyed by fire. St John the Baptist church, built in 1891, burned down on 29 May. Officials have not determined what caused the fire but noted that workmen were in the process of replacing the church’s roof. \xa0
Casey, who was installed as archbishop on 3 April, led a Mass at the nearby Church of the Most Precious Blood the next day.\xa0“When we face times of trial, we know that God can take us on a journey from cross to resurrection,” he told the congregation. He recalled a parish in Florida whose church building was destroyed in a hurricane. They held Mass in the carpark for a year and realised “their true identity as people of God”.

Vatican officials joined delegates from Buddhist universities and monasteries in Cambodia and the Bishops’ Conference of Cambodia for the Eighth Buddhist-Christian Colloquium in Phnom Penh on 27 May. \xa0
Taking the theme “Buddhists and Christians Working Together for Peace through Reconciliation and Resilience”, around 150 Buddhists and Christians from 16 mostly Asian countries participated.\xa0
The prefect of the Vatican’s Dicastery for Inter-religious Dialogue Cardinal George Koovakad told the opening session that “reconciliation and resilience are deeply rooted in our respective faiths and are capable of building and sustaining lasting peace”.\xa0He suggested Christians and Buddhists share spiritual resources for healing people affected by war and injustice.\xa0 
Bishop Olivier Schmitthaeusler, the apostolic vicar of Phnom Penh, emphasised the peaceful coexistence between religions in Cambodia during his welcome address. He thanked the Cambodian government for its support of religious harmony.

The Catholic Bishops’ Conference of India (CBCI) joined the launch of the National Multi-Faith Action Coordination Committee, a national platform to support religious efforts to respond to “the cry of the poor and the cry of the earth” by bringing together multiple faith leaders and faith-based organisations.\xa0\xa0
The CBCI Office for Interreligious Dialogue and Caritas India were represented at the first meeting on 29 May. Alongside them were leaders of seven other faith traditions and representatives of various faith-based organisations working in India. \xa0
They discussed joint coordinated action in key sectors, such as public health and nutrition, water, sanitation and hygiene, child protection and education, and disaster risk reduction and climate action.\xa0The committee decided to hold its next meeting in Delhi to work out an action plan and to meet every quarter to monitor its activities.

Campaigners for international debt relief joined five days of action across 30 countries last week.\xa0They gathered at political landmarks including the UK Treasury, US Congress, and the United Nations headquarters in New York to light candles as part of a global “Relay of Hope” organised by Caritas Internationalis. \xa0
The event called for the cancellation or restructuring of national debt payments for countries unable to afford them, which affect more than 3.3 billion people who live in countries where the government spends more on debt payments than on health or education.\xa0 
The relay linked communities from Brazil to Cambodia and Puerto Rico to South Africa with relay “stages” outside the World Bank Office in Australia and in St Peter’s Square in Rome, as part of the #TurnDebtIntoHope campaign for the 2025 Jubilee.

AFRICA NATIONSDOING BUSINESS IN AFRICAArts, Culture & SocietyMuseums of AfricaFeatures & OpinionLATEST NEWSHeadlinesAMAAPOMOREHAPPENINGENGAGE & LEARNFrom Hollywood to homecoming: Africa’s bold bid to reconnect with the diaspora





 

NG Editor					


 


Arts, Culture & Society, Culture, People 


 

February 13, 2026 Across West and Central Africa, a powerful story is unfolding. From Stevie Wonder receiving Ghanaian citizenship to Ciara becoming a citizen of Benin, African nations are embracing Black American stars as part of a broader reconnection with the diaspora.This is more than celebrity symbolism. It is a strategic, cultural and economic play—rooted in history and powered by a new generation.A bond centuries in the makingThe connection between Africa and African Americans is not new. Liberia, Africa’s oldest republic, was founded in 1822 by freed Black American settlers. When Ghana gained independence in 1957, it welcomed civil rights leaders and artists from the United States. Figures like Martin Luther King Jr., Malcolm X and Muhammad Ali visited Accra, affirming a shared destiny across the Atlantic.Today’s resurgence is shaped by DNA technology, digital storytelling and a rising global African identity. Actors Meagan Good and Jonathan Majors secured citizenship in Guinea after tracing their ancestry there. Rapper Ludacris and actor Samuel L. Jackson are now citizens of Gabon. And YouTube phenomenon IShowSpeed was recently approved for a Ghanaian passport during a whirlwind tour of the continent.For many, these ceremonies are described as “homecomings.” But for African governments, they are also policy.Culture as strategyAt the heart of this movement is pan-Africanism—the belief in a united global African family. Ghana’s “Year of Return” in 2019 positioned the country as a gateway for the diaspora. Over the past decade, more than 1,000 African Americans have relocated there, contributing to real estate, tech startups and cultural ventures.Benin has followed suit. President Patrice Talon has invested heavily in heritage tourism, including the Marina Project in Ouidah, once a major slave port. The goal is clear: transform painful history into spaces of remembrance, renewal and economic opportunity.Tourism is one driver. Diaspora travelers represent a growing and influential market. When global stars share citizenship ceremonies with millions of followers, the ripple effect is immediate—new flights booked, festivals amplified, local brands spotlighted.But soft power matters just as much. Smaller nations without vast mineral wealth are leveraging culture, creativity and identity to expand their global influence. Celebrity citizens, in this sense, become cultural ambassadors—bridges between markets, movements and media ecosystems.A conversation at homeNot everyone is convinced. Some citizens question whether celebrities receive preferential treatment in processes that can be complex for ordinary applicants. Lawmakers in Ghana have emphasized that legal procedures must still be followed, even when certain steps are expedited.This tension reflects something healthy: African nations are not simply staging ceremonies for applause. They are navigating policy, fairness and national identity in real time.And the long-term impact remains to be seen. Will celebrity citizens build businesses, create jobs and deepen cultural exchange? Or will the connection remain largely symbolic?There are early signs of substance. Ciara returned to Benin to perform at Vodun Days. Meagan Good and Jonathan Majors have spoken about establishing lasting ties in Guinea. Ghanaian supporters of IShowSpeed hope he will base projects and investments in the country, extending beyond social media moments.Beyond symbolismAfrica’s engagement with the diaspora is evolving from rhetoric to action. By formalizing citizenship pathways for Afro-descendants, countries are signaling that belonging is not abstract—it can be legal, economic and cultural.This is not about passports alone. It is about rewriting narratives. For centuries, Africa was framed as a place people were taken from. Today, it is positioning itself as a place people return to—by choice.The question is no longer whether the bond exists. It clearly does. The real test is how African nations—and their new celebrity citizens—transform that bond into investment, innovation and shared prosperity.If pan-Africanism is to thrive in the 21st century, it will not be built on speeches alone. It will be built on policy, partnerships and presence.And across the continent, that work has already begun.

 Edo protest: Detained AAU students regain freedom 


                            January 15, 2026 1:38 pm                        




The arrested AAU students. Photo: Edo Police Command





                                By\xa0 Adeyinka Adedipe 


Kindly share this story:

  








 



Getting your Trinity Audio player ready...



 




Students of Ambrose Alli University, Ekpoma, who were arrested and remanded at the Ubiaja Correctional Centre over a recent protest, have been released alongside other detainees.
The release was confirmed in a statement released on Thursday by the Chief Press Secretary to Edo State Governor, Monday Okpebholo, Patrick Ebojele.
The students were among 52 persons remanded by a Benin High Court on charges bordering on malicious damage and armed robbery.
They were arrested after a peaceful protest against insecurity in Ekpoma last Saturday turned violent.  




Hoodlums reportedly hijacked the protest, attacking traders, setting tyres ablaze and blocking roads, scenes which were captured in viral videos on social media.
Okpebholo met with the released students and other affected persons during a stakeholders’ engagement at the Banquet Hall of Government House, Benin, where he assured them of his administration’s commitment to dialogue, reforms and the development of Ambrose Alli University.
The governor said he was deeply pained by the violence, especially given his resolve to revive the university.        
The governor said, “I was highly in pain when I saw what was happening in my place. The day I was sworn in, I said I was going to revive Ambrose Alli University.
“They were paying light bills, water, printing, salaries and everything from ₦41 million, which was not enough for anything at all. But on record, one consultant was receiving ₦59 million under the last administration, while the entire university community was receiving ₦41 million.”
He added that his administration increased the subvention to ₦500m as part of efforts to reposition the institution.
He said, “Since then, it has been about how to revive the school, how to move the school forward and how to reposition it again.”  Related News  May Day: NLC, TUC lament rising insecurity, poverty  Military holding our members over self-defence – Plateau coalition  VIDEO: Policemen filmed assaulting individual in viral clip   
The governor also disclosed that outstanding salary and wage arrears owed to AAU staff stood at about ₦41bn, noting that plans were underway to gradually offset the debt.
He cautioned students against allowing themselves to be used by criminal elements, urging them to focus on their studies and pursue peaceful engagement.
He said, “Somebody said he is a student, and he protests. What are you protesting for? Don’t allow yourself to be used. Read your books so that you can be like us.” 
   
Okpebholo acknowledged the security challenges facing Ekpoma and other parts of the country, describing insecurity as a collective problem.
He, however, said investigations revealed that many criminal activities around Ekpoma were carried out by locals.
The governor condemned the destruction of shops and looting during the protest, describing such actions as counterproductive.
He said, “The same way you are afraid of your life, is the same way I am afraid of my life. Kidnappers do not know the status of anybody, and so it’s a common problem we all have.”
He assured residents and students that criminals would not be allowed to find safe haven in Edo State.
Commissioner for Education, Dr Paddy Iyamu, praised the governor for facilitating the release of the students and choosing dialogue over force.
Other speakers at the meeting also commended the governor’s intervention and commitment to education and peace.        
The meeting was attended by the Deputy Governor, Dennis Idahosa; the Chief of Staff, Gani Audu; the Minister of Youth; the Senior Special Assistant to President Bola Tinubu on Youth Engagement; and other top government officials.

Edo Govt Releases AAU Students Arrested Over Ekpoma Insecurity Protest

















  




 January 15, 2026

 News



 The release was confirmed in a statement issued on Thursday by the Chief Press Secretary to Edo State Governor, Monday Okpebholo, Patrick Ebojele.


 Students of Ambrose Alli University (AAU), Ekpoma, who were arrested and remanded at the Ubiaja Correctional Centre following a recent protest over insecurity in the university town, have regained their freedom alongside other detainees.
The release was confirmed in a statement issued on Thursday by the Chief Press Secretary to Edo State Governor, Monday Okpebholo, Patrick Ebojele.




The students were among 52 persons remanded by a Benin High Court on charges bordering on malicious damage and armed robbery after a protest held last Saturday degenerated into violence.
The demonstration, initially described as a peaceful protest by students against rising insecurity in Ekpoma, was reportedly hijacked by hoodlums who attacked traders, looted shops, burnt tyres and blocked major roads in the town.
Several videos showing scenes of destruction and chaos later went viral on social media.




Following their release, Governor Okpebholo met with the affected students and other stakeholders during an engagement held at the Banquet Hall of the Government House in Benin City.
At the meeting, the governor assured them of his administration’s commitment to dialogue, reforms and the holistic development of Ambrose Alli University.
The governor expressed deep pain over the violence that trailed the protest, especially against the backdrop of his resolve to revive the institution.
Governor Okpebholo said he was deeply troubled by the state of AAU when he assumed office, describing the institution’s funding situation as unacceptable.
He revealed that while the entire university previously received a monthly subvention of ₦41 million to cover salaries, utilities and other expenses, a single consultant was allegedly paid ₦59 million under the last administration.
To reverse the decline, Okpebholo said his government has increased AAU’s monthly allocation to ₦500 million as part of efforts to revive the institution and restore its academic standards. He also disclosed that the university is burdened with about ₦41 billion in unpaid salary and wage arrears, adding that plans are in place to gradually clear the salary arrears.
The governor urged students to avoid violence and not allow themselves to be manipulated by criminal elements, advising them to pursue their grievances through peaceful engagement.
He condemned the looting and destruction of property during recent protests, describing such actions as harmful to the community.
Okpebholo acknowledged the broader insecurity challenges in Ekpoma and across the country, noting that investigations showed many crimes in the area were perpetrated by locals.
He stressed that insecurity affects everyone equally and pledged that his administration would not allow criminals to find refuge in Edo State.
Commending the governor’s approach, Education Commissioner Dr. Paddy Iyamu praised his decision to prioritise dialogue over force and for facilitating the release of detained students, describing it as evidence of responsive and people-centred leadership.
On Tuesday, the governor ordered the release of protesters who are genuine AAU students, arrested during the protest against kidnapping in Ekpoma.
The governor stated that the protest was not organised by AAU students, describing the incident as a criminal act carried out by non-students hiding around the institution to foment trouble and extort law-abiding students.
The governor disclosed this during a meeting with the AAU’s Students’ Union Government (SUG) President, Osadebamen Ehizogie Michael, according to a statement signed by Ebojele.
“Intelligence available to us shows that the incident was orchestrated by a group identifying themselves as the ‘Ambrose Alli Comrade Community,’ made up of individuals who are no longer students but loiter around the school environment for unlawful activities,” the statement said.
“Any genuine students who may have been arrested in the course of restoring order would be identified and released without delay.”
“For students who were probably arrested, we will look into it and get them released to the SUG President. That is a promise I made,” the statement added.

Edo Governor Okpebholo Orders Release Of Protesting 52 AAU Students After Backlash

















  




 January 14, 2026

 News



 According to him, students had consistently maintained their innocence and cooperated fully with university authorities in clarifying their position.


 
The Edo State Governor, Monday Okpebholo, has ordered the release of protesters who are genuinely students of Ambrose Alli University (AAU), arrested during the protest against kidnapping in Ekpoma, Esan West Local Government Area.
The governor on Tuesday claimed that the protest was not organised by AAU students, describing the incident as a criminal act carried out by non-students hiding around the institution to foment trouble and extort law-abiding students.
The governor disclosed this in a statement signed by his spokesperson, Patrick Ebojele, on Tuesday during a meeting with the AAU’s Students’ Union Government (SUG) President, Osadebamen Ehizogie Michael.
“Intelligence available to us shows that the incident was orchestrated by a group identifying themselves as the “Ambrose Alli Comrade Community,” made up of individuals who are no longer students but loiter around the school environment for unlawful activities.
“Any genuine students who may have been arrested in the course of restoring order would be identified and released without delay.
“For students who were probably arrested, we will look into it and get them released to the SUG President. That is a promise I made,” he said.
SaharaReporters had reported that the SUG denied any involvement of students in the recent protest that occurred in Ekpoma community, distancing the university community from claims that students organised or participated in the unrest.
Speaking on Tuesday in a video obtained by SaharaReporters, the Student Union President, Osadebamen Ehizogie Michael, said the protest was neither organised under the aegis of the Student Union Government nor the National Association of Nigerian Students (NANS), describing attempts to link the incident to students as “pitiable and misleading.”
“Contrary to popular claims, the protest was not organised under the Student Union Government or NANS,” Osadebamen said. “It is quite unfortunate that some persons are deliberately trying to associate what transpired with the student community. We are here to officially debunk that claim. Students did not organise any protest.”
He appealed to the Edo State Government and security agencies to act decisively to ensure the immediate release of students arrested in connection with the incident, insisting that they had no hand in the protest.
According to him, students had consistently maintained their innocence and cooperated fully with university authorities in clarifying their position.\xa0
Corroborating the SUG’s position, a senior university official said investigations carried out by the institution showed that those who organised and carried out the protest were not students of Ambrose Alli University.
“As the Student Union President rightly said, that protest was organised by a group of people who are not our students,” the official said. “The law will take its course. However, for our students who were arrested, we appreciate His Excellency for his promise that they will be released as soon as possible.”
The official added that the university management had engaged with the affected students, all of whom denied participating in the protest.\xa0
He disclosed that the school convened a roundtable dialogue involving relevant stakeholders, but individuals from the community allegedly responsible for the protest failed to attend.
“We invited all parties for dialogue, but the so-called community members did not turn up,” the official said. “From our findings, whatever happened in Ekpoma was not part of any student plan. It was carried out by individuals who refused to leave the Ekpoma community.”
SaharaReporters had reported how no fewer than 52 students of Ambrose Alli University were remanded in a correctional facility following a midnight crackdown by operatives of the Nigeria Police Force on student hostels across the school.\xa0
The students were arrested late at night from their hostels and subsequently charged to court for allegedly participating in a protest held last Saturday against rising cases of kidnapping and worsening insecurity in the state.
Students who spoke to SaharaReporters said the arrests were carried out in a coordinated operation, with police officers storming several hostels in the middle of the night and picking up students indiscriminately.
According to them, many of those arrested were not apprehended at the protest venue but were picked up from their residences.
“They came to our hostels at night and started arresting students,” one of the affected students told SaharaReporters from the court premises. “Many of those arrested were sleeping in their rooms and were not even on the streets when the protest took place.”\xa0
On Monday, the arrested students were arraigned before a court in Edo State, where the police filed a motion seeking their remand in custody.\xa0
Documents obtained by SaharaReporters show that the police applied for an order to remand the 52 students for an initial period of 14 days, pending legal advice from the Director of Public Prosecution (DPP), Edo State Ministry of Justice.
The motion was brought pursuant to Sections 293(1) and 296(1) of the Administration of Criminal Justice Law of Edo State, 2016, and under the inherent jurisdiction of the court.
The application, dated January 12, 2026, requested the court to remand the students at the Benin City Correctional Custodial Centre while investigations continue.
The arrests followed a mass protest by residents of Ekpoma over rising cases of kidnapping and insecurity in the area.
During the protest, angry residents pulled down campaign billboards mounted across the town ahead of the 2027 general elections, accusing politicians of prioritising early campaigns over the safety of residents.

FacebookTwitterPinterestWhatsApp




Benin, Feb. 20, 2026 (NAN) The Edo Government says it has demolished a building in Amagba, Oredo Local Government Area, where two members of the Aye confraternity were arrested in connection with recent cult killings in the state.
This is contained in a statement by Dr Patrick Ebojele, the Chief Press Secretary to Governor, on Thursday in Benin.

“The Special Security Squad also sealed off a residence at Upper Uwa area of Benin, the last known residence of Etiosa Akhiombare Joshua, also known as Baba Josh.
“Joshua, is a suspected member of the Maphite Confraternity, and mastermind and financier of last week’s killing at Wire Road,” he said. (NAN)

  Special security squad smashes cult syndicate in Benin

By The Nigerian Observer 
July 21, 2025




























… Illegal arms recovered as govt intensifies crackdown on criminal gangs



BENIN CITY – In a major breakthrough in the fight against cultism and violent crime, the Edo State Government’s Special Security Squad, Operation Flush Out Kidnappers and Cultists, has arrested several notorious cult leaders and recovered dangerous weapons in coordinated raids across Benin City.        
You May Have Missed

                            2027 race: Ex-council boss Ilaboya joins race for Owan West Assembly seat

                            Safety beyond hard hats: WSO Nigeria pushes for mental wellbeing                        



The operation was disclosed in a statement issued at the weekend by Fred Itua, Chief Press Secretary to Governor Monday Okpebholo, who described the crackdown as part of the state government’s sustained efforts to ensure public safety and security.



Acting on intelligence, the team stormed various hideouts on Friday evening, including Iguedaiyi village in the Egor Local Government Area, where a suspected cult leader was apprehended and weapons were recovered during a search of his residence.\xa0     
The raid was part of the broader mission to rid Edo State of criminal elements threatening public peace.



Two previously arrested suspects reportedly provided vital information that led to the discovery of other cultist hideouts, where additional weapons and dangerous materials were seized.        
You May Have Missed



                            Can Nigeria eliminate malaria before 2030?                        



                            World Malaria Day: LAPO distributes free drugs, rallies women on prevention                        



                            2027 race: Ex-council boss Ilaboya joins race for Owan West Assembly seat                        



                            Turaki-led PDP faction loses as Supreme Court voids Ibadan convention                        



                            Safety beyond hard hats: WSO Nigeria pushes for mental wellbeing                        



The security squad, comprising operatives from the Nigerian Army, Police, NSCDC, hunters, vigilante groups, and repentant cultists was inaugurated by Governor Okpebholo to tackle insecurity head-on and dismantle criminal networks across the state.



A member of the squad, Ameloemen Idemudia Noah, explained that the ongoing operations are intelligence-led and devoid of political motivations, refuting claims intended to discredit the effort.



“We are guided by professionalism and community engagement. The people are with us, and their support has been instrumental,” Noah said.     
He stressed that while the team’s primary mandate is to neutralise cultists and kidnappers, other crimes uncovered during operations will be prosecuted by relevant security agencies.



“Our aim is to restore peace. Criminality must end in Edo State. The killings and insecurity must stop,” he added.



Itua, in his statement, reiterated the administration’s zero-tolerance policy on criminality, affirming that Governor Okpebholo remains resolute in his commitment to make Edo State safe for all residents.

                            2027 race: Ex-council boss Ilaboya joins race for Owan West Assembly seat                        



There is no gainsaying that the return of Benin artefacts is more than museums shipping boxes to Nigeria. It is history coming home, spiritual healing for a people whose ancestors were robbed, and a practical step toward fairness in global culture. Many people in the West, including museum directors, government ministers, scholars, and activists, made hard choices and sacrifices to do the right thing. Many Nigerians — from His Royal Majesty, the Oba of Benin and\xa0 the National Commission for Museums and Monuments (NCMM) leaders to local funders and artists — organized, negotiated, and prepared to welcome the artefacts home.\xa0



For Nigerian youths, seeing the original Benin bronzes they have hitherto been reading about on the pages of textbooks, on websites, blogs and the social media, now physically in Benin City, gives you your history back. It is something to be proud of, learn from, and build new art and stories from. For artists and tech creators: These objects are fuel for new art, films, music, and digital projects that can connect Nigeria to global audiences on equal terms. For the world: Returning stolen culture shows that countries can admit wrongs and fix them. It sets a global model for justice, respect, and partnership. This shared effort shows how respect, honesty, and care can rebuild bonds between peoples and make the world a fairer place for cultural heritage.     
As The Nigerian Observer feels the pulse of the people it directly affects, Nigerians are baring their minds on the real import of what is about to happen in their very eyes: Mr. Isaac Asemota of Semo Gallery told The Nigerian Observer that when the artifacts were still abroad, it was used by the westerners as a source of generating revenue. And economically speaking, he suggested that if the artifacts were used in the same way it was in the western world, it is going to be beneficial to the Nigerian economy in the long run. But he ended by expressing his lack of confidence in the Nigerian system. In his words, “will they be used that way for the good of the people?”



‎Mr. Okah, a\xa0 visitor to Owie Gallery, an art shop in Benin City, said that the artifacts were items of spiritual significance and that issues relating to it should be managed by the Palace. In his words, “I can’t casually talk about the artifacts because, for me it belongs to the Palace”. He continued: “The economic effect of the artifacts on our economy solely depends on the Governor of Edo State and the Benin Monarch”.\xa0

                            Safety beyond hard hats: WSO Nigeria pushes for mental wellbeing                        



                            2027 race: Ex-council boss Ilaboya joins race for Owan West Assembly seat                        



‎Mr. Tyson Oghenetejiri, an artist popularly called “Coffee Boy” had a rather peculiar view of the matter; speaking with him he stated, “Culturally, the true value of the Benin artefacts was revealed to us only after the West recognized and showcased them. Their prestige abroad forced us to reflect:\xa0 “If our ancient works could generate pride, scholarship, and revenue for them, then they must carry immense value for us too. Ironically, the theft awakened our awareness of what we had”. “Coffee Boy” said the effect he felt these returned artifects would have on the economy and future of Nigeria. He said, “Economically for Nigeria, the artefacts now represent not only heritage but also potential tourism revenue, global attention, and a rebranding of our image. But the challenge lies in whether we can preserve, maintain, and present them with the dignity they deserve.”



\xa0The contemporary artist ended by saying, “My philosophy is that everything happens for a reason. If those works had not been taken, I doubt if we would still have many of them intact today. Their survival came at a painful cost, but it preserved a part of our soul. So yes, they were stolen, but they were also safeguarded. We owe it to history, and to ourselves, to now guard them with gratitude and responsibility.”



Another respondent, Don Pedro, an artist, posited that the return of the Benin artifacts to Nigeria holds profound cultural and economic significance, shaping the nation’s future in several ways such as reclaiming identity and history: The Benin Bronzes are not merely art objects they are historical records and symbols of the pre-colonial Kingdom of Benin. Their return allows Nigeria and the Edo people specifically, to reclaim their narrative, history, and cultural ownership     
‎National Pride and Inspiration: The repatriation process itself is a source of national pride, representing a successful effort to right historical injustices. The physical presence of these masterpieces can inspire a new generation of Nigerian artists, historians and citizens. It will build a renewed appreciation for their heritage and a sense of cultural continuity.



‎The returned artifacts are crucial for cultural restoration in the sense that they can be used for public exhibitions, educational programs and research therefore allowing Nigerians to engage directly with their history. The Economic Significance it has to the future of Nigeria is that it will be a Boost to Tourism in that\xa0 The return of the Benin Bronzes will draw for a major cultural tourism By housing these world-renowned artifacts in a new museum, such as the planned Museum of West African Art (MOWAA), Nigeria can attract international and domestic visitors. This will create a new economic ecosystem, with potential for job creation in hospitality, transportation, and related industries. Also he said Museum The need to properly house and preserve the returned artifacts will increase investment in museum infrastructure, staff training and exhibition design. Furthermore he stated that Economic Empowerment through The development of a vibrant cultural sector around the returned artefacts can lead to economic empowerment for local communities and for artist too.



‎ Mrs. Mairo another respondent added that these artifacts will help to restore our lost cultural heritage and would remind other countries that Nigeria will ultimately regain her lost glory.

Nigerian soldiers still in Burkina Faso after forced aircraft landing – Foreign Affairs Minister





Published on December 11, 2025



By Matthew Atungwu 







 

















The Minister of Foreign Affairs, Yusuf Tuggar, says the Nigerian soldiers who were on an aircraft that made a forced landing in Burkina Faso are still trapped in that country.
Tuggar made this disclosure during a press briefing with his Beninese counterpart, Olushegun Bakari, on Thursday at the ECOWAS Commission in Abuja.
Recall that on Monday, the Confederation of Sahel States (AES) accused an aircraft carrying 11 Nigerian soldiers of violating Burkinabe airspace.







DAILY POST reports that AES is a breakaway West African regional union made up of Burkina Faso, Mali and Niger Republic.
The Mali junta leader, Assimi Goita, described the landing as an unfriendly act carried out in defiance of international law.
The AES said it authorised its member states to neutralise any aircraft violating its airspace.
The development came at the same time Nigerian troops carried out air strikes in Benin to help foil a coup.
Commenting on the situation, the Nigerian Air Force, NAF, said the C-130 aircraft was on a ferry mission to Portugal.

on Monday, December 8, after allegedly violating its airspace.The development was disclosed by Agence d’Information du Burkina, the state-owned news agency, which published a statement issued by the Confederation of Sahel States (AES), a bloc comprising Burkina Faso, Mali and the Niger Republic.In the statement, the AES said investigations by Burkinabe authorities highlighted the absence of authorisation to fly over the territory of Burkina Faso for this military device.The regional bloc condemned the incident in strong terms, describing it as a violation of its sovereignty and that of its member states.FG intensifies efforts to get soldiers releasedIn a statement issued by the director of Public Relations and Information, Air Commodore Ehimen Ejodame, the Nigerian Air Force said the crew diverted to the nearest airfield in Bobo-Dioulasso, Burkina Faso, in line with international aviation safety protocols.Read alsoFG gives fresh update on soldiers held in Burkina Faso after forced landingEjodame added that the detained personnel were safe and receiving cordial treatment from Burkinabe authorities.Meanwhile, the federal government has intensified diplomatic efforts to secure the release of both the soldiers and the aircraft.Speaking with The PUNCH on Wednesday, the spokesperson for the Ministry of Foreign Affairs, Kimiebi Ebienfa, said Nigeria’s embassy in Ouagadougou was actively engaging Burkina Faso’s authorities to resolve the issue.“The Embassy of Nigeria in Ouagadougou is engaging with the host authorities to secure their release,” Ebienfa said.Reaction trails Nigerian soldiers' detention in Burkina FasoHowever, the situation has sparked debate at home, with a former federal lawmaker, Ayodele Arise, urging President Bola Ahmed Tinubu to consider stronger measures.Arise, a chieftain of the All Progressives Congress (APC) and former senator representing Ekiti North, made the call while speaking on Arise TV.Burkina Faso keeps Nigerian soldiers in its custody for six days consecutively.

Photo credit: @YusufTuggar Source: TwitterTuggar maintained that the incident complied with International Civil Aviation Organisation (ICAO) protocols and expressed confidence that the matter would be resolved soon through diplomatic channels.NAF: Landing was due to technical issueThe Nigerian Air Force explained that the aircraft was on a ferry mission to Portugal when the crew observed a technical fault that required a precautionary landing in Bobo-Dioulasso, Burkina Faso.NAF spokesperson Ehimen Ejodame said the action aligned with standard international aviation procedures.He emphasised that the landing was not linked to the political situation in the Benin Republic, where Nigerian troops had assisted in thwarting a coup attempt.#Man observes where Air Force jet is headedA Nigerian man has reacted to the alleged arrest of some Nigerian soldiers in Burkina Faso and made an unexpected statement.Read alsoFG begins diplomatic push to secure release of detained aircraft, troops in Burkina FasoHe mentioned the alleged destination of the Air Force jet that flew over Burkina Faso, and many individuals reacted to it.He also shared a screenshot in his post to support his statement, as the incident continued to spark online conversations.Benin Republic presidency speaks after attempted coupLegit.ng earlier reported that military personnel in Benin announced on December 7 that they had removed President Patrice Talon from office. The declaration was made on state television by a group calling itself the “Military Committee for Refoundation” (CMR). The soldiers said they had met and decided that “Mr Patrice Talon is removed from office as president of the republic.” Shortly after the announcement, the broadcast signal was cut. Proofreading by James Ojo, copy editor at Legit.ng. Source: Legit.ng

                                By\xa0 Adeyinka Adedipe

The Edo State Police Command has arrested 95 suspects over renewed cult clashes that have claimed several lives in the past two weeks in the state.
In a statement released on Saturday, the Command spokesman, Moses Yamu, said that 64 of the suspects have been charged to court and remanded, while investigation continues on the remaining suspects.
He said raids which were carried in Benin and its environs also led to the recovery of firearms while those arrested belong to the Maphites, Eiye, and Aye confraternities, who are battling for supremacy.
The statement read, “The Edo State Police Command has arrested 95 suspected cultists and recovered two locally made pistols, one single barrel shotgun and 24 live cartridges in sustained intelligence driven raids of their enclaves in Benin and environs for the past week.

“64 of the suspects have been charged to court and remanded, while investigation continues on the remaining suspects.”

He warned youths in the state to desist from joining unlawful societies that could only dim their future and ultimately claim their lives or that of other innocent\xa0persons.

No fewer than 95 suspected cultists have been arrested by the Edo State Police Command following deadly clashes among members of the Maphites, Eiye, and Aiye confraternities.

“These suspects, who belong to the notorious Maphites, Eiye, and Aiye confraternities, were suspected to be responsible for the cult supremacy killings in the state.”        
The Commissioner of Police, Monday Agbonika, advised parents and guardians to keep tabs on the activities of their wards to curb cultism.

Police arrest 95 suspected cultists, recover arms, ammunition in Edo





Published on May 3, 2025



By Enahoro Iyemefokhai 







 

















Operatives of the Edo State Police Command have arrested 95 suspected cultists and recovered arms and ammunition.
DAILY POST reports that the Public\xa0 Relations Officer of the Command, Moses Yamu disclosed this in a statement made available to DAILY POST, weekend, in Benin City.
Yamu said the suspects were members of the notorious Maphites, Eiye, and Aye confraternities.







He alleged that they have been responsible for the cult supremacy killings in the State.
He said out of the 95 suspects, 64 of them have been charged to court and remanded, while investigation continues on the remaining suspects.
The Public Relations Officer listed items recovered from the suspects to include two locally made pistols, one single barrel shotgun, and 24 live cartridges.
He added that the suspects were arrested in sustained intelligence-driven raids of their enclaves in Benin City and environs for the past week.
The Commissioner of the Command, Monday Agbonika, however, urged parents and guardians to keep a tab on the activities of their wards to curb the menace of cultism.
